notable.notable_blurb: Designer-owner of the SP-1228 USS Acoma, a wooden-hulled

burial.plot_notes: Pine Grove Cemetery, Leicester MA (Worcester County)

narrative_blocks.header: Designed his own twin-screw motor boat, 1917
narrative_blocks.body: He designed a 59-foot wooden-hulled twin-screw motor boat himself and submitted the plans to the U.S. Government. He paid for the Acoma's construction at Gas Engine & Power Co. and Charles L. Seabury Co. of Morris Heights NY in 1917 — 59 feet long, 11 feet 8 inches beam, 21.9 knots cruising speed, complement of 9.

narrative_blocks.header: Donated his boat to Navy on free lease
narrative_blocks.body: He donated the Acoma to the Navy on free lease 25 September 1917; she was commissioned 18 October 1917 as USS Acoma SP-1228 and assigned to the Second Naval District, armed with one 1-pounder gun and one machine gun. She patrolled Newport RI and then New Bedford CT through the end of WWI.

narrative_blocks.header: Returned two weeks after Armistice, retained boat
narrative_blocks.body: The Acoma was returned to him on 25 November 1918 — two weeks after the Armistice — since he had funded her construction. He served as Boatswain's Mate First Class during the war and was promoted to Ensign in April 1919. He later lived in Pasadena CA and died in Riverside CA at eighty-six.

notable.notable_blurb: College of Physicians and Surgeons NY 1875

landmarks.blurb: Principal organizer of 1909 NYS legislation creating Bear Mountain State Park; Palisades Interstate Park Commissioner 1913-1930.

narrative_blocks.header: Organized creation of Bear Mountain State Park, 1909
narrative_blocks.body: As a part-time Cornwall-on-the-Hudson resident he enlisted Edward H. and Mary Harriman, Lyman Abbott, Henry Fairfield Osborn, George Kunz, Herbert Satterlee, and Ernest Stillman to petition for federal Hudson Highlands national-park status. When that failed they shifted to New York State and won 1909 legislation creating Bear Mountain State Park.

narrative_blocks.header: Seventeen years on Palisades Park Commission
narrative_blocks.body: He was appointed to the Palisades Interstate Park Commission in 1913 and reappointed for five-year terms in 1915, 1925, and 1930. During his terms Bear Mountain was extended by nearly 50,000 acres and Storm King Highway was constructed largely from a land grant of Ernest G. Stillman.

narrative_blocks.header: Manhattan obstetrician with five hospital appointments
narrative_blocks.body: After his 1875 MD from the College of Physicians and Surgeons (now Columbia) he served more than twenty years in NYC general practice and as professor at the same school. He was visiting or consulting physician at New York Hospital, the Nursery and Child's Hospital, Sloane Maternity Hospital, and the New York Infant Asylum.

landmarks.blurb: Wife of Edward Lasell Partridge; died 1907, two years before Bear Mountain State Park was established.

narrative_blocks.header: Died before her husband's Bear Mountain victory
narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of Theodore William Dwight founder of Columbia Law School, she married the NYC obstetrician Edward Lasell Partridge in 1884 at age 28. She died at Cornwall-on-Hudson NY in 1907 at fifty — two years before her husband's lobbying campaign succeeded in creating Bear Mountain State Park.

orbit_connections.name: Grover Cleveland (X00770) — taught by Elliott Anthony who studied under Dwight
orbit_connections.relationship: Anthony (who married Theodore's sister) taught Cleveland before studying law under Theodore Dwight

narrative_blocks.detail: In 1858 Theodore William Dwight accepted an invitation to develop a law department at Columbia College. He was the sole professor until 1873 when it expanded into Columbia Law School. The "Dwight method" — memorization of treatises, practice drills, frequent moot courts — competed directly with the case method developed by Christopher Columbus Langdell at Harvard. Columbia Professor Peter Strauss described the contrast: "Where Dwight aimed to give a sound knowledge of the law to men of average ability, Harvard's case method aimed to give as much intellectual stimulation as possible to those who would become the profession's elite." In 1891 Dwight led faculty, students, and alumni out of Columbia in protest of trustees' attempt to convert to the case method, and co-founded New York Law School. A column on Columbia's College Walk at 116th and Amsterdam is dedicated to him.
narrative_blocks.body: Built Columbia's law department from scratch in 1858; sole professor for 15 years; dean until 1891. Led the faculty walkout over the Harvard "case method" controversy and co-founded New York Law School the same year.

narrative_blocks.header: Studied physics under Samuel F.B. Morse
narrative_blocks.detail: Theodore William Dwight graduated Hamilton College in 1840 as a member of Alpha Delta Phi, having studied physics under Samuel F.B. Morse (who the following year would demonstrate long-distance telegraphy). Beyond legal education, Dwight was a prominent prison reformer: he collaborated on A Report on Prisons and Reformatories in the United States and Canada (1867), served as president of the New York Prison Association, was delegate to the International Prison Congress at Stockholm in 1878, helped draft the bill establishing the Elmira Reformatory, and wrote the early report leading to the organization of the State Charities Aid Association. He also served as counsel for five Andover Theological Seminary professors charged with heresy in 1886.
narrative_blocks.body: At Hamilton College he studied under Samuel F.B. Morse and John William Draper. As a reformer he helped draft legislation for the Elmira Reformatory, served as president of the New York Prison Association, and attended the International Prison Congress in Stockholm (1878).

Elected Professor of Municipal Law at Columbia College in 1858, he organized the Columbia Law School with thirty-five pupils in its first year and was made its Warden. Under his sole instruction the school grew to 425 students by 1874 — his style of recitation-and-lecture pedagogy with weekly moot court drew national attention.

After 33 years at Columbia he resigned in 1891 and that same year founded New York Law School with several Columbia colleagues — making him a founder of two major American law schools. He died the following year in 1892 at his Clinton NY family home.

narrative_blocks.header: Won state law admitting law graduates to bar
narrative_blocks.body: Before Columbia he taught law at Hamilton 1846-1858 and procured passage of a New York statute admitting his Hamilton Law School graduates to practice on the basis of their diploma alone. When the Supreme Court declared the statute unconstitutional he won reversal at the Court of Appeals — the argument was published as a thick pamphlet by Hamilton's trustees.

institutions.blurb: Hamilton College BA 1840 (Alpha Delta Phi); tutor 1842-1846; Professor of Law, History, Civil Polity, and Political Economy 1846-1858; Dean of Hamilton Law School 1853.
Founder + first Warden of Columbia Law School 1858; grew school to 425 students by 1874
Founder 1891 — the year before his death

narrative_blocks.header: Family added the 'a' to Olmstead after move
narrative_blocks.body: Born Mary Bond Olmsted in Plainfield MA in 1823, daughter of Asa Olmsted. In the 1840s her family moved to Clinton NY where her father started using the surname with an added 'a' as 'Olmstead' to match other Clinton-area families. She married Theodore William Dwight in 1847 and bore three children before his Columbia Law career began.
